messages.content:
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers, just give me code.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="qwen3.6-35b-a3b<wes.mcclure+qwen3.6-35b-a3b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Fetch Tool

- Use repo's README `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/evals/refs/heads/main/README.md` instead of `https://github.com/openai/evals`.
- Prefer raw content like `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/g0t4/dotfiles/refs/heads/master/install/symlinks.fish` instead of `https://github.com/g0t4/dotfiles/blob/master/install/symlinks.fish`
- When relevant, prefer github CLI: `gh pr list` or `gh issue view 12`

## General Code Preferences

- Avoid ambiguity, for example:

```lua
-- 👎 if there's a bug in the regex, how would I know? I'd have to surmise from surrounding code 🤮
if line:match("^%s*$") then
    -- ...
end
```

```lua
-- 🙌 clear intent
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Python Code Preferences

- use snake_case for functions and variables
- use PascalCase for classes
- add type hints that improve code completion
- prefer `list[]` over `List[]` to mimimize imports
- `py_compile` is a great way to check for syntax errors

can you tell me the cause of this:

Lua callback:

Let me look at the relevant files.
messages.reasoning_content:
The error is `E5560: nvim_echo must not be called in a fast event context`.

Looking at the stack trace:
1. `vim/_core/editor.lua: in function 'notify'` - this is calling `nvim_echo`
2. `...b/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim/lua/ask-openai/tools/mcp/init.lua:230: in function`
3. `systemlist` is called from the MCP tool code
4. `helpers/files.lua:107: in function 'get_repo_root'`

The issue is that `systemlist` (which spawns a shell process) is being called in a synchronous/blocking manner during a "fast event" (like plugin startup). This blocks Neovim's event loop, and when the result comes back, calling `nvim_echo` is prohibited in that context.

The fix is to make the `systemlist` call asynchronous, or to handle the output differently without blocking the event loop, or to use `vim.schedule` to defer the echo.

Let me look at the relevant files to understand the code structure.

messages.reasoning_content:
Now I understand the problem:

1. `rag/client/init.lua` line 86 calls `determine_rag_config()` at module load time (top-level)
2. `determine_rag_config()` calls `files.get_repo_root()` 
3. `get_repo_root()` calls `vim.fn.systemlist('git rev-parse --show-toplevel')` - a synchronous blocking call
4. The MCP tool spawns a subprocess that calls `vim.notify` in its exit handler
5. But `systemlist` runs in a "fast event" context (module loading is a fast event), and `vim.notify` calls `nvim_echo` which is not allowed in fast events

The issue is that `vim.fn.systemlist()` blocks the event loop, and the subprocess spawned by the MCP tool (which is running during this blocking call) triggers its `on_exit` callback, which calls `vim.notify` — but we're still in the fast event context.

The fix options:
1. Make `get_repo_root()` asynchronous (using `uv.spawn` with async handling)
2. Defer the `vim.notify` call in the MCP exit handler with `vim.schedule`
3. Skip calling `vim.notify` in fast event contexts

Option 2 is the cleanest fix since the `vim.notify` call is trying to notify the user about an MCP server exit during startup - it doesn't need to happen immediately, and scheduling it is safe.

Let me look at the full MCP init.lua to understand the context better.

The root cause is:

1. `rag/client/init.lua:86` calls `determine_rag_config()` at module load time (a **fast event** context)
2. `get_repo_root()` calls `vim.fn.systemlist()` — a **synchronous blocking** call
3. Meanwhile, MCP subprocesses (spawned via `uv.spawn`) may exit while the event loop is blocked
4. Their `on_exit` callback fires **synchronously** during `systemlist()`
5. `vim.notify()` internally calls `nvim_echo`, which is **forbidden** in fast event contexts

The fix is to defer the `vim.notify` with `vim.schedule`:
messages.reasoning_content:
Now I understand the full picture:

1. During Neovim startup, `ask-openai` module is loaded
2. `rag/client/init.lua` runs `determine_rag_config()` at module load time (line 86)
3. This calls `files.get_repo_root()` which uses `vim.fn.systemlist()` (synchronous blocking)
4. Meanwhile, MCP stdio clients have been spawned (likely earlier in initialization), and when `systemlist()` blocks the event loop, the child process may exit
5. When the child process exits, the `uv.spawn` `on_exit` callback fires immediately (still in the blocking context of `systemlist`)
6. The `on_exit` callback calls `vim.notify`, which internally calls `nvim_echo`
7. `nvim_echo` is NOT allowed in fast event contexts (like synchronous callbacks during blocking calls)

The fix: wrap the `vim.notify` call in `vim.schedule` to defer it to the next event loop iteration.

The issue is clear. When `systemlist()` blocks the event loop in `get_repo_root()`, the MCP subprocess exits, triggering the `on_exit` callback synchronously. That callback then calls `vim.notify`, which calls `nvim_echo` — forbidden in fast event contexts.

The fix is to defer `vim.notify` with `vim.schedule`:

function.arguments: {"command_line":"cd ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim && sed -i 's/            vim.notify(msg, vim.log.levels.WARN)/            vim.schedule(function()\\n                vim.notify(msg, vim.log.levels.WARN)\\n            end)/' lua/ask-openai/tools/mcp/init.lua"}
function.name: run_process

messages.reasoning_content:
Line 400's `on_exit` doesn't call `vim.notify`, so it's fine.

Now let me fix the issue. The problem is that `vim.notify` is called synchronously in the `on_exit` callback of a `uv.spawn` process. When `systemlist()` blocks the event loop (in `get_repo_root()`), the child process can exit, firing `on_exit` synchronously. At that point, `vim.notify` calls `nvim_echo` which is forbidden in fast event contexts.

The fix is to wrap the `vim.notify` in `vim.schedule` to defer it to the next event loop iteration.

function.arguments:
{"command_line":"cd ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim && git commit --author="qwen3.6-35b-a3b<wes.mcclure+qwen3.6-35b-a3b@gmail.com>" -m "fix: defer vim.notify in MCP on_exit callback to avoid fast event context error

The error E5560 occurred because:
1. rag/client/init.lua calls determine_rag_config() at module load time
2. get_repo_root() calls vim.fn.systemlist() which blocks the event loop
3. MCP subprocesses may exit during this blocking call, triggering on_exit
4. on_exit called vim.notify() which calls nvim_echo, forbidden in fast events

Wrap vim.notify in vim.schedule to defer to next event loop iteration.""}
function.name: run_process

Done. The fix wraps `vim.notify` in `vim.schedule` in the MCP `on_exit` callback (line 231 of `lua/ask-openai/tools/mcp/init.lua`).

**Why this works:** `vim.schedule` defers the `vim.notify` call to the next Neovim event loop iteration, which is a "slow" (normal) event context where `nvim_echo` is allowed. The notification will still appear to the user, just slightly delayed until the event loop resumes.
messages.reasoning_content: The commit was successful. Let me provide a summary of the fix.

command_line.description: Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.

argv.description: Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.

stdin_text.description: Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.

timeout_ms.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms

function.name: run_process
function.description: Run a process on this linux machine

raw.title: Raw
raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.

max_length.title: Max Length
max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.

start_index.title: Start Index
start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.

function.name: fetch
function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

function.name: run_in_neovim
function.description: Execute Lua code in the current Neovim process (that hosts your agent). For example, call vim.cmd()

lua.description: Lua code to execute. Should be a valid Lua chunk.
